title: Dynamic Entanglement Packet Scheduling for Quantum Networks
authors: Quang-Phong Tran, Claudio Cicconetti, Marco Conti, Andrea Passarella
abstract: Sharing entanglement among multiple users remains a central challenge for scalable quantum networks. Recent work proposed an on-demand entanglement packet architecture in which a controller uses a Time Division Multiple Access (TDMA) approach to allocate network resources. Quantum nodes are assigned a periodic schedule that probabilistically fulfills application requests for end-to-end entanglements. The schedule is recomputed periodically using well-known algorithms, such as Earliest Deadline First (EDF). However, a static schedule offers limited flexibility when outcomes are stochastic and arrivals are asynchronous. To overcome this limitation, we propose an online scheduler that dynamically schedules, defers, retries, or drops entanglement distribution reservations. In our simulations, the dynamic scheduler achieves lower completion time, higher completion ratio, and higher throughput than the static baseline. Furthermore, when the network is overloaded, the dynamic scheduler continues to construct deadline-feasible schedules and degrades gracefully.
summary: This paper proposes a dynamic scheduling algorithm for quantum networks to replace inefficient static schedules. By reacting to real-time successes and failures of entanglement generation, the scheduler improves network throughput and reliability. It is particularly effective at managing resource contention in high-load scenarios.
main_problem: Efficiently sharing entanglement among multiple users in scalable quantum networks is difficult due to stochastic entanglement generation outcomes and asynchronous request arrivals, which cause static TDMA schedules to fail.
main_result: The proposed dynamic online scheduler achieves higher throughput, lower completion time, and a higher completion ratio than static EDF-based baselines, while demonstrating graceful degradation under network overload.
method: The authors develop an online, event-driven scheduler that manages two priority queues (Event and Ready) to dynamically decide whether to admit, defer, retry, or drop entanglement distribution reservations based on real-time outcomes.
model_or_system: A time-slotted quantum network model using an undirected graph where nodes perform entanglement generation and swapping. The architecture uses an on-demand 'entanglement packet' approach with Packet Generation Attempts (PGAs) across paths.
key_observables: Completion ratio, throughput, makespan, admission rate, link utilization, and waiting time.
important_parameters: Target success probability (p_packet), link generation probability (p_gen), Bell State Measurement success probability (p_bsm), application period (T_a), and path length (number of hops).
main_assumptions: Entanglement generation is triggered only upon request; no multipartite entanglement is considered; routing is fixed to the shortest path; and the model assumes instantaneous entanglement swapping and simplified quantum memory.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a 5-node network topology; Figure 2 provides a toy example comparing static vs. dynamic scheduling success/failure; Figure 3 shows static admission rate vs. load; Figure 4 compares performance metrics (completion ratio, makespan, etc.) between the two schedulers; and a final figure shows the impact of path length on packet drops and retries.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the entanglement packet architecture, defines the mathematical network and application models, presents the proposed dynamic scheduling algorithm, describes the simulation setup using the GARR network, and concludes with a performance comparison and discussion of future work.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. A quantum network with a central controller that coordinates 5 nodes. Each has a certain number of quantum links.

Fig. 2. A toy example comparing the static (top) and dynamic (bottom) schedulers, where 3 applications a request one entanglement packet (∀a ∈A, Ia = 1) with periods Ta. 2 applications arrive at t0, while the last arrives at t6. Each scheduler schedules PGAa,k (time budgets), represented by solid double arrows. In each slot, an attempt of E2E entanglement is performed (entanglement generation and swapping with all the links of the selected path πa). Each color is associated with an application and an outcome represented by a symbol. The hyper-periods Hi are used only by the static scheduler. The dashed double arrows indicate specific runtime mechanisms of the dynamic scheduler (defer and...
Fig. 3. The static scheduler admission rate of feasible schedules vs. ppacket.

Fig. 4. Comparison between the static and dynamic schedulers: the left figure shows the completion ratio (top) and makespan (bottom) in logarithmic scale vs. ppacket; the right figure shows the 90th/95th percentile per-link utilization (top) and per-link waiting time (bottom) vs. ppacket.

Fig. 5. Performance on the load and the path length of the dynamic scheduler: the left figure shows the completion ratio and throughput according to an increasing load for different values of ppacket; the right figure shows the proportion of PGAs completed/dropped and fractions of deferred/retried based on the number of hops (for napps = 200, ppacket = 0.3). Note that a PGA can be deferred and retried as long as it can meet its deadline.
